steps.title: Find the axis (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a vertical line down the middle of where the crock will sit. The crock is symmetric, so this axis is your reference for keeping both sides even.

steps.title: Block the body (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, sketch the silhouette around the axis: a soft barrel shape, wider at the belly and narrower at the top and bottom. Both sides should mirror each other across the axis.

steps.title: Mark the rim and base (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, add a shallow ellipse along the top opening and a flatter one at the base. These tell the viewer the crock is a cylinder, not a flat cutout.

steps.title: Refine the curves (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, look at the curves of your silhouette. Where they feel too uniform, erase and lay down a fresh stroke. Most natural pottery curves tighten near the rim and base and loosen out around the belly.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the silhouette
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace the body of the crock and the front of the rim ellipse in confident passes. The rim's back half can stay implied — let the front edge do the work.

steps.title: Ink the rim detail (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, add a single curved line just below the rim to suggest a thick lip. One stroke wrapping around the top gives the crock its weight.

steps.title: Add a contents hint (ink)
steps.description: Add two or three short marks rising just above the rim — handles of wooden utensils, perhaps. A few small ticks is all it takes to suggest the crock is in use.
